questions.prompt: Why validate on the server in addition to (or instead of) the client?

choices.text: Server validation is authoritative — it's the security boundary. Malicious actors can bypass any client-side validation by calling the action directly or modifying client code. Server-side validation also works when JavaScript fails (progressive enhancement). Client-side validation is fine as a UX enhancement to give instant feedback without a round-trip, but the server validates again on submit. Use the same Zod schema in both for consistency; one source of truth. Pattern: server is the truth, client is the polish.

questions.explanation: Server validation is authoritative (security + progressive enhancement). Client is UX polish.

questions.prompt: Building a 'like' button — toggling should feel instant but write to the DB. How?

choices.text: Use `useOptimistic` to update the UI immediately before the server confirms. Set the optimistic state to the new value, fire the Server Action, and React reverts if it fails. Pattern: `const [optimisticLiked, setOptimisticLiked] = useOptimistic(post.liked, (s, newState) => newState)`. Click handler sets the optimistic value then awaits the action. The button shows the new state instantly; the server catches up. Use for simple, easily-reversible mutations like likes, toggles, and adds. Don't use for complex multi-step operations where rollback would confuse.

questions.explanation: useOptimistic for instant UI feedback with automatic revert on failure.
